chapters.title: Glucose is a source of energy, but its excess may lead us to trouble
chapters.text:
The main thing about food in the 21st century is it’s easily accessible. We eat whatever we like and however we want it: sea products from all over the world and meat delivered from the finest farm households. We are free to choose, but somehow, we make the wrong choices.

Food affects our physical and mental health, and it is more than a source of energy. While the nutritional part was decisive for people thousands of years ago, we want our food carefully served and presented today. Aside from the aesthetics and taste, how much do we know about the food we consume?

The body speaks to us all the time, and what we put in our mouths impacts 30 trillion cells and 30 trillion bacteria within us. Chronic fatigue, mood shifts, heart disease, and diabetes are the most common messages telling people something is wrong.

Scientific studies show that there is a core metric influencing all systems in the human body, which is often perceived as a black box. This metric is a molecule called glucose, aka blood sugar. It's a primary source of energy that makes sure the cells can properly function.

If you could plot a glucose level every minute, the line between peaks and valleys would shape a glucose curve. Jessie Inchauspe explains why flattening this curve is critical for staying healthy and losing weight. Being curious about our health issues, Inchauspe joined the genetic startup, 23andMe, where she learned genes don’t decide what kind of mood you’ll be in after waking up; they can only tell if you’re likely to have diabetes or cancer. So far, glucose remains one of the few measurable points you can take to regain control of your life.

It’s hard to know what’s going on inside a human body; if our feelings had a voice, we might have a chance to explain them.

Glucose Revolution provides insight into glucose and why the so-called spikes (a sudden increase of glucose to more than 30 mg/dL) are unhealthy. It gives life hacks like eating foods in the proper order or drinking vinegar before meals to stay within the normal range of glucose.

chapters.title: Plants use glucose combinations to build their trunk parts
chapters.text:
Where does the glucose come from? Millions of years ago, when our planet was a compilation of water and mud, tiny sprouts began a life-ensuring process called photosynthesis. They used the sun's energy to turn carbon dioxide and water into glucose. Even though people argued that plants were made of dirt, a Flemish scientist, Jan Baptist van Helmont, found a way to disprove the hypothesis. In the 1640s, he planted a baby willow tree, and after five years, discovered the weight of the soil in the pot did not change, while the tree grew pretty high.

To be fair, plants don’t spend time at a grocery store; they have all the resources to create their food right away with no additional appliances. Once created, plants either keep glucose as a building block or use it for energy. It would be nice for humans to have a superpower of inhaling pure air and then, let’s say, creating a soup in their stomachs. But we have in common with plants — we need glucose to keep our cells functioning.

Humans consume glucose just as plants do with one tiny difference — we cannot synthesize it out of plain water.

Plants use glucose to build other chemical compounds, packing lots of molecules hand-to-hand (creating starch) or hand-to-feet (creating fiber). Starch is an energy reserve located primarily on the roots; fiber makes plants stronger and taller and is found in the trunk and leaves. Fiber is like the grout between bricks — it's critical to ensure a house can withstand storms. Some of the plants’ glucose transforms into an extra-sweet molecule called fructose, making the plant irresistible to animals. When animals eat fruits with seeds, they spread those seeds far and wide, thus ensuring the plant's survival.

The result of combining glucose and fructose is sucrose. For plants, sucrose is convenient storage because its molecules are smaller than glucose, so they can squeeze to keep more energy in a tighter space. For people, it goes by the name of table sugar.

chapters.title: In the present time, fruits are over sweet and have much less fiber
chapters.text:
To live a happy life, humans need fuel in massive amounts. In 1969, scientists launched Tentative Rules for Carbohydrates Nomenclature, presenting a new family of substances to the scientific community. They included a separate group of sugars (glucose, fructose, and sucrose) as the primary energy sources alongside starch and fiber as more advanced molecules.

While glucose is the primary source of energy, fructose does not play a part in energy supply. But fiber has a different fate. Unlike starch and sucrose, the bonds of fiber can’t be snapped by enzymes, so this food material passes nearly untouched through the digestive system. Try to find fiber on supermarket shelves. Companies often remove it on purpose because its presence is questionable regarding product preservation, but they increase sweetness with artificial sugar to attract potential customers and make them return.

Considering ancestral bananas, we see them full of fiber and with little sugar. But today’s banana results from hundreds of breedings to intensify its taste. It’s a vivid example of how, once people find something worthy of attention, they have to take it to an extreme level.

Sugar is now highly concentrated and freely available: we went from eating a fibrous food in prehistoric times to packing ourselves with chocolate bars every single day.

Ever wondered why we like sweetness so much? Dopamine is to blame. Back in the Stone Age, finding a fruit meant a significant advantage. Its sweet taste was a sign the food was safe and filled with energy. We evolved in many prospects, but we still feel pleasure when tasting something sweet.

Luckily, we’re good in terms of balancing micronutrient levels. Doctors call it metabolic flexibility. When there is a rise in blood sugar, the pancreas releases a hormone called insulin. It's responsible for stashing excess glucose in storage units to keep it out of circulation. When the sugar supply is low, many cells (except for the red blood cells — they always rely on glucose) can switch to using fat for fuel. At the same time, the liver uses amino acids and fat to make glucose from internal resources. This process is called gluconeogenesis. Therefore, even though it can’t create glucose directly, the body has its way of coping with non-standard situations.

chapters.title: During a spike, glucose gets to the cells quickly, bringing us closer to the state of inflammation
chapters.text:
Jessie Inchauspe discovered a device called Continuous Glucose Monitor (CGM) to measure her glucose activity during the day. A CGM measures glucose concentration between the fat cells. She applied it to the back of her hand and wore it for two weeks. Although a standard blood sugar range is between 60 and 100 mg/dL, according to ADA, the best range for fasting glucose (a level we have first thing in the morning) is from 72 to 85 mg/dL.

The body needs to eliminate excess glucose from our bloodstream by keeping it elsewhere. There are three central locations for storage: the liver, muscles, and fat (only the last applies to fructose). Since insulin plays a part in storing sugar, it helps maintain an optimal glucose range. However, even non-diabetics can still experience glucose spikes.

Here are the reasons why an excessive number of sweet products is harmful to your health:
• They initiate fructose spikes that are not visible and measurable, but it doesn’t mean they are less harmful.
• They cause oxidative stress; if the glucose we consume exceeds the amount our cells need, they release free radicals in response. Those are destructive substances poking holes in membrane cells and modifying our DNA (so we can be more susceptible to cancer if the genes are broken).
• They damage molecules directly.

In 1912, a French chemist, Louis-Camille Maillard, described a reaction when glucose bumps into another type of molecule. It's called glycation and causes direct damage to the cell. If lots of molecules and cells in the liver, for example, are damaged, doctors will diagnose liver failure. And if this happens to a bone (a rib cage usually), it will turn brown, albeit very slowly.

Glycated molecules have long-term consequences, including Alzheimer's disease, cataracts, and heart disease.

The mentioned factors lead to a generalized state of inflammation. Chronic inflammation is the source of chronic illnesses that reduce life quality even at a young age. You may see redness from the outer part, while tissues and organs get slowly damaged on the inner side.

chapters.title: A way to introduce yourself to proper nutrition is by changing the order of food you consume
chapters.text:
People focus on which products to eat and which to avoid. Instead, they should be focusing on how they consume food. The correct order is fiber first, fat and protein second, and carbs last. Here’s why: sugars get to the small intestine quickly, and from there, they enter the circulation, causing a glucose spike. It leads to a massive release of insulin and significantly lowers blood sugar levels. That’s when you feel tired, crave another snack, or long for an afternoon nap.

What's bad about insulin spikes? In addition to a glucose level crash, we tend to experience side effects, such as shakiness, light-headedness, or tingling in the hands and feet. Insulin also turns off fat-burning mode, thus preventing hyperglycemia (the medical term for elevated blood sugar). As a result, insulin reduction is mainly primordial and precedes weight loss.

The amount of carbs and the speed of eating is decisive for the size of the glucose spike.

If you eat fiber first, it will:
• Reduce the impact of alpha-amylase (an enzyme that breaks down starch to glucose).
• Delay the process of gastric emptying (moving food from the stomach to the small intestine).
• Form a viscous mesh within the intestine walls slowing down glucose absorption.

Fiber helps strengthen the microbiome in the gut and lowers cholesterol levels. The US government calls fiber a “nutrient of public concern,” saying only 5% of Americans meet the recommended amount: 25 grams per day.” So, the golden rule is to add fiber-rich products (for example, a green salad) to your meal routine and delay consuming carbs as late in the meal as possible.

Any vegetable, from roasted asparagus to grilled zucchini, means additional calories. But you won't be afraid of them if you understand the types of calories you eat. Health and weight have to do more with the molecules, which in this case are the so-called “healthy” products that have fewer calories but cause more damage to the metabolism. Organizing a meal is a proven way to stay fit even if your daily calorie count exceeds 2000.

Did you know? According to the scientific studies conducted by Cornell University in 2015, eating items in the correct order reduces a glucose spike by 73% and an insulin spike by 48%.

chapters.title: Sugary breakfast is one of the common beliefs we must debunk
chapters.text:
For many people, breakfast consists of starch and tons of sugar. What do we usually eat? A “healthy” cereal with orange juice? Nutella crepe? That's precisely what Jessie Inchauspe had for breakfast before leaving for school.

We are vastly susceptible to glucose in a fasted state, which is why eating a sugar-filled breakfast leads to a higher spike and is the main reason we will be hungry soon after. In addition, the rest of the day will depend on the glucose curve created by the first meal we eat. As a result, you might feel dizzy and sleepy during lunchtime.

A spiky breakfast is a ticket to a glucose roller coaster.

We think the sweeter our food is, the more energy it brings. However, diets with more carbohydrates lead to less available energy in the bloodstream. Fruit juice, for instance, is a worse option than whole fruit because making juice involves pulverizing fiber into tiny particles, which are no longer able to perform a protective function. Eating takes longer than drinking, so you will curb the glucose spike if you eat one apple instead of drinking apple juice.

One of the popular myths says that fruits rot in the stomach if consumed with another type of food. But there is no scientific evidence supporting this because rotting takes weeks, and the acidic environment in the stomach would not allow this to happen.

While fruits are needed for better health, they still cause spikes. You may have heard the expression “lower glycemic index." It refers to products spiking glucose levels less, but there’s still a fructose spike you might not notice. Agave syrup is the best example — doctors recommend it for diabetic patients without considering the therapeutic pitfalls.

To flatten the glucose curve, choose a savory option. An ideal alternative for breakfast is one containing a solid amount of protein, fiber, and fat. The same applies to your midday snacks: choose apple slices with nut butter or a cup of 5% Greek yogurt topped with a handful of pecans. It will help curb your cravings, increase your energy, and sharpen mental clarity.

chapters.title: A perfect combo for having a sweet snack is “vinegar before, exercise after”
chapters.text:
There are plenty of factors determining how we feel: sleep, exercise, stress level, emotional connection, and medical care. It's hard to say whether a particular food is good or bad because it is all relative. For example, high-fiber pasta may sound nice compared to regular pasta, but it’s certainly a “bad” choice with respect to veggies.

If you seriously want to flatten the glucose curve (if you have constant cravings, are exhausted in the morning, or happen to be glucose intolerant), it’s helpful to add extra vinegar to your shopping list.

The impact of this recipe is hard to underestimate: pour a tall glass of water, add one tablespoon of 5% vinegar in it, and drink 20 minutes before, after, or during a meal. Among the common varieties of vinegar, the apple cider variant is the most popular. Scientists proved that drinking vinegar before food helps lower triglyceride levels and reduce visceral fat along the waist and hips. It doesn’t cause an insulin spike as insulin injections do.

Vinegar works in two ways:
• The acetic acid in it inactivates alpha-amylase, slowing down the arrival of glucose into the circulation.
• It encourages the muscles to compose glycogen (analog of starch in humans), which leads to less efficient glucose uptake.

As a pleasant surprise, vinegar also reprograms DNA to burn fat faster. It happens in microscopic organelles inside the cells called mitochondria. The mitochondria use glucose and oxygen to create an authentic version of electricity to pass to each cell. They are like a car engine; without them, we would be stuck on the roadside asking for fuel.

Vinegar is not a justification for eating more sugar: it is a helpful addition to your daily routine when you cannot replace carbs with protein or fiber.

When muscle cells lack fuel, they need glucose molecules to burn, and the energy muscles create from glucose is packed in adenosine triphosphate (ATP) molecules. The good news is you can use it to flatten the glucose curve.

Let's assume you eat foods in the proper order and always put fiber first. What else to do? Try a short walk after a meal (about 10 minutes). The muscles will use more glucose, automatically decreasing the blood sugar level. Therefore, a simple change of eating habits and exercise will help you get more energized.

chapters.title: Combining glucose with other molecules makes the body receive it at a more natural speed
chapters.text:
When we finish a meal, we start a new activity, but the party's just getting started for our bodies. The postprandial state (a medical term for post-eating) marks the most significant changes in hormonal levels and inflammation. The more glucose and fructose spikes we experience, the more demanding the postprandial state is for the body. In the 21st century, we spend nearly 20 hours in the post-eating period daily, while back in the 1980s, this figure was twice as low because people didn’t snack between meals, satisfying themselves with breakfast, lunch, and dinner.

Eating larger meals is a way to increase your metabolic flexibility. With no need to snack every one or two hours, your body will use fat as fuel instead of glucose. If you still can’t resist something sweet to eat, leave it for dessert to follow the correct food order and please your dopamine receptors.

If we forgo snacks, the internal organs will have more time to repair their lost cells and do a general cleanup of the systems.

The other hack to prolong a post-eating state is intermittent fasting. Beware, this is not a universally suitable hack, and before you start, schedule an appointment with a doctor. If fasting for 6, 8, or 12 hours does not apply to you, try lessening your calorie count for a few days.

All the mentioned tips and tricks sound good, but what to do in a 9-5 schedule when you have limited options for lunch and even less space to exercise? First, never go for starch or sugar alone. Whatever you eat, try to combine carbs with fat, protein, or fiber. Instead of letting carbs reach your intestine naked, put some “clothes” on them, such as dressing. Enjoy a brownie at a friend's place but eat Greek yogurt with it; have a bagel at the meeting, but put some lox on the side. If you don't follow the order for your food, the absorption of carbs will be much slower.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Sugar is the best we can get out of food; that’s one way to explain our cravings for carbs. Glucose gives energy to our cells; it regulates the fat-burning process; its lack triggers dopamine-dependent zones in the brain. On the other hand, this molecule can be toxic, putting cells into oxidative stress and causing damage to our DNA. So, how to start regulating your blood sugar for good?

Through her deep scientific research, Jessie Inchauspe votes for the following statement: carbs alone cannot coexist in peace with our inner laboratory. They need fat, protein, and fiber to make every cell perform its function without a need to recharge our battery every few hours. A single chocolate bar is, in fact, far from a reliable energy source.

However, some of the described tricks need a careful review. For example, advice to drink vinegar may be crucial for people with a low level of acid in the stomach, so before making any changes in your diet, you need to do a medical checkup.

People who have type 1 diabetes (when the pancreas can’t produce insulin) or type 2 diabetes (when the body is resistant to insulin) should also be careful. If they are administered a sugar-dropping medication, they need to count the glucose level in an hour or so. Trying out new hacks is good, but the sky's the limit, so there is no need to lower blood sugar all the way down to hypoglycemia.

Despite the body’s internal systems constantly sending messages, we often perceive the body as a black box full of mysteries. Thus, bringing objective scientific studies on metabolism into the realm of action is the best we can do to acknowledge those messages. On the contrary, let's separate evidence-based techniques from the pseudoscientific claims floating around.

Try this

• Ensure your breakfast has protein in it. Add some Greek yogurt, tofu, meat, fish, cold cuts, and of course, eggs to your regular menu.
• Stay in the outside aisles in the grocery store; the inner ones mostly contain starchy and sugary foods.
• Consider green starters that are both quick and healthy: roasted veggies, sliced cucumber with guacamole, a sliced tomato with mozzarella cheese, or baby carrots with hummus.

Read it till the end?
